title: -Ordinance Vacating a Portion of Carleton Street from Westerly Terminus Extending 381.23 Feet Easterly

sections.recommendation:
1. Conduct a public hearing and upon conclusion, adopt the first reading of an
Ordinance vacating a 381.23-linear-foot (27,449 square foot) portion of the public
right-of-way on Carleton Street, located at the westerly terminus of Carleton
Street.
2. Schedule a public hearing and second reading of the Ordinance for the July 29,
2025, Council meeting.
3. Upon conclusion of the second reading of the Ordinance, authorize the City
Manager to execute a deed quit claiming that portion of the right-of-way of
Carleton Street and conveying to the adjacent property owner, Bayer Healthcare
LLC ("Bayer").
sections.fiscal_impacts:
There is no negative fiscal impact to the City of Berkeley from adopting this ordinance.
Bayer has paid the appropriate fees related to processing this street vacation. In
addition, the street vacation would have a positive financial impact as it will reduce long-
term City maintenance and public service costs as well as increase property tax due to
increase of the Bayer-owned parcel.
sections.current_situation:
The 381.23-linear-foot segment of Carleton Street proposed for vacation, as described
in Exhibit A (attached) and shown on Exhibit B (attached), is a dead-end right-of-way.
Bayer owns all properties adjacent to the proposed vacated section, including on the
north, south, and west sides of the subject roadway segment. Therefore, Bayer
submitted a request for the City to vacate the dead-end portion of Carleton Street to
improve internal circulation and enhance facility security.
The City is currently responsible for maintaining this segment of Carleton Street. In
addition, the City is liable for any incidents and accidents that may occur in this section

of the right-of-way. By vacating this segment, the City eliminates maintenance

Carleton Street eastward to improve site circulation and security.
sections.background:
Pursuant to Division 9, Part 3, Chapter 3 of the Streets and Highways Code of the State
of California, a local agency may vacate a public right-of-way of a street or highway
when the street is unnecessary for present or prospective public street uses. The
Streets and Highways Code allows the initiation of public street vacation by the City
Council by the adoption of a Resolution of intention, the fixing of a date for a public
hearing, and the publication and posting of notice as required therein. The Council
adopted the Resolution of intention on July 8, 2025, for this purpose.
ENVIRONMENTAL SUSTAINABILITY AND CLIMATE IMPACTS
Vacating a portion of Carleton Street decreases the amount of right-of-way available to
motor vehicles which reduces the amount of pollution they produce. Similarly,
maintenance activities that may have negative environmental impacts, such as street
repaving and concrete sidewalk replacement, would be reduced.
sections.rationale:
The Public Works Department has reviewed the proposed vacation pursuant to the
California Streets and Highways Code §§ 8320, 8322, 8323, 8324 and determined that
the segment is "unnecessary for present or prospective public use." This segment of the
right-of-way is not identified as (1) a major, collector, or local residential street in the
General Plan; (2) as a current or future part of the City's bicycle network in the Bicycle
Plan; (3) as a primary or secondary transit route; or (4) as an emergency evacuation
route. This portion of Carleton Street provides access to only Bayer's campus, and the
vacation of this portion of the street is similar to others the City has adopted in the
immediate area in connection with Bayer's campus.
City staff also determined that the vacation will not negatively impact emergency
services or utility access. Bayer will preserve the right of the City of Berkeley and utility
providers including but not limited to Pacific Gas & Electric Company (PG&E), East Bay
Municipal Utility District (EBMUD), and American Telephone and Telegraph Company
(AT&T) to maintain, repair, and perform other activities related to their infrastructure in
the vacated right-of-way per Section 8340 of the California Streets and Highways Code.
In addition, approval of this vacation allows the City to avoid maintenance costs and
liability on a portion of Carleton Street that provides access to only one property owner
(Bayer).
sections.alternatives:
The alternative course of action is to deny the request for street vacation. The City
would continue to be responsible for maintaining this portion of Carleton Street and hold

liability for any incidents that may occur despite this area being surrounded on three
sides by Bayer-owned properties.
CONTACT PERSON
Ronald A. Nevels, Manager of Engineering, Engineering Division (510) 981-6439
Vincent Chen, Supervising Civil Engineer, Engineering Division (510) 981-6409

Ordinance Vacating a Portion of Carleton Street from PUBLIC HEARING
Westerly Terminus Extending 381.23 Feet Easterly July 22, 2025
ORDINANCE NO. –N.S.
VACATION OF A PORTION OF CARLETON STREET BETWEEN THE WESTERLY
TERMINUS OF CARLETON STREET AT THE BAYER CAMPUS AND A POINT
APPROXIMATELY 381.23 FEET EASTERLY THEREOF
BE IT ORDAINED by the Council of the City of Berkeley as follows:
Section 1. Vacation of Portion of Right-of-Way
That pursuant to Streets and Highways Code Section 8320 et. seq., the City of Berkeley
hereby vacates the following portion of the public right-of-way of Carleton Street located
at the westerly terminus of Carleton Street at the Bayer Campus, more specifically
described in Exhibits A and B, on the basis of the following findings:
a. That the public right-of-way described above is a dead-end street providing street
access to one single property, that being the Macaulay Foundry located at 811 Carleton
Street;
b. That the owner of the Macaulay Foundry site, Bayer Healthcare LLC, has
requested the City vacate that portion of Carleton Street providing access to the Macaulay
Foundry site, such owner having established legal access to public streets through
property located west and adjacent to it;
c. That the portion of the right-of-way described in Exhibits A and B is not identified
as a present or prospective major, collector, or local residential street in the City's General
Plan;
d. That the portion of the right-of-way described in Exhibits A and B is not a current
or future part of the City's bicycle network in the Bicycle Plan;
e. That the portion of the right-of-way described in Exhibits A and B is not identified
as a present or prospective primary or secondary transit route in the City's General Plan;
f. That the portion of the right-of-way described in Exhibits A and B is not identified
as a present or pros
